stem: Solve the initial value problem \( \frac{dy}{dx} = [[a]]x^{2}y \), \( y(0) = [[y0]] \), and evaluate the solution at \( x = [[t0]] \).
solution: Separate variables: \( \frac{dy}{y} = [[a]]x^{2}\,dx \), so \( \ln|y| = \frac{[[a]]x^{3}}{3} + C \) and \( y = C_1 e^{[[a]]x^{3}/3} \). The condition \( y(0) = [[y0]] \) gives \( C_1 = [[y0]] \), hence \( y(x) = [[y0]]\,e^{[[a]]x^{3}/3} \). At \( x = [[t0]] \) this equals \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by 3 when integrating \( [[a]]x^{2} \).
This treats the equation as \( y' = [[a]]y \), ignoring the \( x^{2} \).
This drops the initial value \( [[y0]] \), leaving \( C_1 = 1 \).
This uses the wrong sign in the exponent.
This integrates \( x^{2} \) as if it were \( x \), giving \( x^{2}/2 \)-type growth.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Solve the nonlinear separable equation \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} = [[a]]y^{2} \), \( y(0) = [[y0]] \), and evaluate the solution at \( x = [[t0]] \).
solution: Separate variables: \( \dfrac{dy}{y^{2}} = [[a]]\,dx \), so \( -\dfrac{1}{y} = [[a]]x + C \). The condition \( y(0)=[[y0]] \) gives \( C = -\dfrac{1}{[[y0]]} \), so \( y(x) = \dfrac{[[y0]]}{1-[[a]]\cdot[[y0]]x} \). At \( x=[[t0]] \), \( y = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This mistakes the equation for the LINEAR \( y'=[[a]]y \), giving exponential growth instead of the correct rational blow-up form.
This drops the \( y_0 \) factor from the numerator.
This uses the wrong sign in the denominator.
This uses a single Euler step (a linear approximation) instead of the exact solution.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The curve \( y=y(x) \) (with \( y>0 \)) satisfies the separable equation \( y\,\dfrac{dy}{dx} = [[a]]x \), with \( y(0) = [[y0]] \). Evaluate \( y([[t0]]) \), taking the positive branch.
solution: Separating gives \( y\,dy = [[a]]x\,dx \), so \( \dfrac{y^{2}}{2} = \dfrac{[[a]]x^{2}}{2} + C \). The condition \( y(0)=[[y0]] \) gives \( C = \dfrac{[[y0]]^{2}}{2} \), so \( y(x)^{2} = [[a]]x^{2} + [[y0]]^{2} \). Taking the positive branch, \( y([[t0]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to square \( x \) before scaling by \( [[a]] \).
This forgets the square root, leaving \( y^{2} \) instead of \( y \).
This takes the negative branch instead of the positive one.
This treats the relation as linear in \( x \) rather than quadratic under the root.
This uses a linear (tangent-line) approximation instead of the exact implicit solution.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: A quantity \( y(t) \) with \( 0<y<1 \) grows logistically according to \( \dfrac{dy}{dt} = [[a]]y(1-y) \), \( y(0) = \dfrac{[[num]]}{[[den]]} \). Evaluate \( y([[t0]]) \).
solution: By partial fractions, \( \int\left(\dfrac1y+\dfrac1{1-y}\right)dy = \ln\left|\dfrac{y}{1-y}\right| = [[a]]t + C \), so \( \dfrac{y}{1-y} = Ae^{[[a]]t} \) with \( A = \dfrac{y_0}{1-y_0} = [[A]] \). Solving for \( y \), \( y(t) = \dfrac{Ae^{[[a]]t}}{1+Ae^{[[a]]t}} \), so \( y([[t0]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This ignores the saturation term \( (1-y) \), treating the growth as plain exponential.
This uses the wrong sign in the exponent, growing toward the wrong limit.
This forgets to normalize by the denominator \( 1+Ae^{at} \).
This places \( A \) in the numerator instead of \( Ae^{at} \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: A colony of bacteria grows at a rate proportional to its current size, so its size \( P(t) \) (in thousands) satisfies \( \dfrac{dP}{dt} = [[k]]P \). If \( P(0) = [[y0]] \) thousand, find \( P([[t0]]) \) (in thousand bacteria).
solution: Separating variables, \( \dfrac{dP}{P} = [[k]]\,dt \), so \( \ln P = [[k]]t + C \) and \( P(t) = C_1e^{[[k]]t} \). The condition \( P(0)=[[y0]] \) gives \( C_1=[[y0]] \), so \( P([[t0]]) = [[y0]]e^{[[k]]\cdot[[t0]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats the growth as linear rather than exponential.
This adds the rate term instead of exponentiating it.
This uses the wrong sign in the exponent, giving decay instead of the correct trend (or vice versa).
This multiplies \( t \) outside the exponential instead of inside it.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
